text: The surgeon approaches the thymic tumor from the left side using thoracoscopy, with the patient positioned supine. Three ports are utilized: two for working and one for the camera, with an optional fourth port for retraction. The phrenic nerve is visualized alongside the thymic tumor. Carbon dioxide gas is used at a pressure of nine to ten millimeters of mercury to facilitate the procedure. Adhesiolysis is performed to separate adhesions between the tumor and the lung, ensuring preservation of the phrenic nerve to maintain diaphragm functionality.
The surgeon carefully dissects the parietal pleura over the mediastinum, located just anterior to the phrenic nerve, while taking precautions to avoid thermal injury to the nerve.
The surgeon makes an anterior incision along the anterior reflection of the pleura, causing the anterior mediastinal contents to drop down towards the thymic specimen.

The surgeon carefully secures the thymic vessels, with particular attention to the thin veins that drain into larger veins such as the brachiocephalic vein. The innominate vein is visible beneath the ligated vessel, highlighting the need for careful handling to prevent tearing and potential bleeding.

The surgeon carefully dissects the phrenic nerve away from the thymic tumor, ensuring it remains free from invasion. The pericardial tissue is excised along with the tumor specimen. The right pleura and the formation of the superior vena cava are visible as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon identifies the left horn of the thymus and the clearly defined innominate vein. The thymic horns are dissected or pulled down from the neck, with careful attention to avoid bleeding from the superior thymic vessels, which are ligated meticulously.
The surgeon dissects and reflects the entire anterior mediastinal fat, removing it along with the thymectomy specimen.

The posterior thymic vessels, direct branches from the arch of the aorta, are visible. The floor of the thymus is exposed following the completion of dissection. The arch of the aorta and the pulmonary trunk are clearly defined, along with the phrenic nerve.
The surgeon extracts the thymectomy specimen through a sub-xiphoid incision, using a wound protector to minimize pain. Bilateral chest drains are placed following the extraction.
